title: The Idyl of Battle Hollow
content:
آ آ آ آ No, I wonï؟½t, thar, now, so! And it ainï؟½t nothinï؟½, no!
آ آ آ آ And tharï؟½s nary to tell that you folks yer donï؟½t know;
آ آ آ آ And itï؟½s ï؟½Belle, tell us, do!ï؟½ and itï؟½s ï؟½Belle, is it true?ï؟½
آ آ آ آ And ï؟½Wotï؟½s this yer yarn of the Major and you?ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Till Iï؟½m sick of it all, so I am, but I sï؟½pose
آ آ آ آ Thet is nothinï؟½ to you. . . . Well, then, listen! yer goes!

آ آ آ آ It was after the fight, and around us all night
آ آ آ آ Thar was poppinï؟½ and shootinï؟½ a powerful sight;
آ آ آ آ And the niggers had fled, and Aunt Chlo was abed,
آ آ آ آ And Pinky and Milly were hid in the shed:
آ آ آ آ And I ran out at daybreak, and nothinï؟½ was nigh
آ آ آ آ But the growlinï؟½ of cannon low down in the sky.

آ آ آ آ And I saw not a thing, as I ran to the spring,
آ آ آ آ But a splintered fence rail and a broken-down swing,
آ آ آ آ And a bird said ï؟½Kerchee!ï؟½ as it sat on a tree,
آ آ آ آ As if it was lonesome, and glad to see me;
آ آ آ آ And I filled up my pail and was risinï؟½ to go,
آ آ آ آ When up comes the Major a-canterinï؟½ slow.

آ آ آ آ When he saw me he drew in his reins, and then threw
آ آ آ آ On the gate-post his bridle, and what does he do
آ آ آ آ But come down where I sat; and he lifted his hat,
آ آ آ آ And he says well, thar ainï؟½t any need to tell that;
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas some foolishness, sure, but it ï؟½mounted to this,
آ آ آ آ Thet he asked for a drink, and he wanted a kiss.

آ آ آ آ Then I said (I was mad), ï؟½For the water, my lad,
آ آ آ آ Youï؟½re too big and must stoop; for a kiss, itï؟½s as bad,
آ آ آ آ You ainï؟½t near big enough.ï؟½ And I turned in a huff,
آ آ آ آ When that Major he laid his white hand on my cuff,
آ آ آ آ And he says, ï؟½Youï؟½re a trump! Take my pistol, donï؟½t fear!
آ آ آ آ But shoot the next man that insults you, my dear.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Then he stooped to the pool, very quiet and cool,
آ آ آ آ Leavinï؟½ me with that pistol stuck there like a fool,
آ آ آ آ When thar flashed on my sight a quick glimmer of light
آ آ آ آ From the top of the little stone fence on the right,
آ آ آ آ And I knew ï؟½twas a rifle, and back of it all
آ آ آ آ Rose the face of that bushwhacker, Cherokee Hall!

آ آ آ آ Then I felt in my dread that the moment the head
آ آ آ آ Of the Major was lifted, the Major was dead;
آ آ آ آ And I stood still and white, but Lord! gals, in spite
آ آ آ آ Of my care, that derned pistol went off in my fright!
آ آ آ آ Went off true as gospil! and, strangest of all,
آ آ آ آ It actooally injured that Cherokee Hall!

آ آ آ آ Thetï؟½s all now, go ï؟½long! Yes, some folks thinks itï؟½s wrong,
آ آ آ آ And tharï؟½s some wants to know to what side I belong;
آ آ آ آ But I says, ï؟½Served him right!ï؟½ and I go, all my might,
آ آ آ آ In love or in war, for a fair stand-up fight;
آ آ آ آ And as for the Major sho! gals, donï؟½t you know
آ آ آ آ Thet Lord! tharï؟½s his step in the garden below.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Bret Harte (Francis) represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Bret Harte (Francis)'s bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
